description: Proximal 'ha' points to what the speaker treats as near: this person, this place, this time, or the matter at hand. Before a noun it selects a nearby or current referent. Standing with 'nai' (be), it is the location predicate here; its partner 'ra' (DIST) points beyond the immediate frame.
articulatory_notes: Air passes through an open glottis for 'h' and continues into unrounded open-central 'a'. Full stress covers the one syllable.
sound_symbolism: Because the breath and vowel arrive almost together, 'ha' can feel immediate. The association makes a modest mnemonic for the nearer form.
usage_notes: Within Slot 2, 'ha' follows 'we' (ALSO) or 'li' (RESTR) and precedes plurality, numerals, focus, and degree. The same form covers number: 'ha melu' means this friend, while 'ha lo melu' means these friends. Phi divides deixis into proximal and distal, without a separate medial form.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The phrase 'ha philo' (today) uses the same particle as 'mia ha nai' (I am here). Present day and present place meet in ordinary speech.
